title: if, else if, and else Statements

explanation.title: Making Decisions in Java with if, else if, and else

codeExample.title: Student Grade Classifier
codeExample.explanation:
- `if (score >= 90)` — condition in parentheses; body in braces; both are required in Java
- `else if (score >= 80)` — evaluated only when all previous conditions were `false`
- `String grade, feedback;` — both variables declared before the if-chain; they must be assigned in every branch
- `grade = "B"; feedback = "Great work!";` — assigning inside a branch; Java's compiler verifies every branch assigns both variables
- `score >= 50 ? "PASS" : "FAIL"` — ternary; evaluates to `"PASS"` when condition is true, `"FAIL"` otherwise

exercise.title: Write a Cinema Ticket Price Calculator
exercise.instructions:
Write a Java program that determines cinema ticket price based on age. Use if/else if/else with these rules: under 5 = free (price 0), 5 to 12 = Rs 100, 13 to 17 = Rs 150, 18 to 59 = Rs 200, 60 and above = Rs 120. Declare int age = 25 and int ticketPrice. Assign ticketPrice in the branches. Print age and ticket price with labels.

Expected output with age 25:
Age: 25
Ticket Price: Rs 200
hints: 💡 Add else if (age <= 17) { ticketPrice = 150; } after the age <= 12 branch.
💡 Add else if (age <= 59) { ticketPrice = 200; } for adults.
💡 The final else covers age 60 and above — no condition needed since all lower ages are already handled.

questions.question: What is the correct Java syntax to start an if statement?
options: if condition {
if (condition):
if (condition) {
IF (condition) {
questions.explanation: Java's if statement syntax requires parentheses around the condition and curly braces around the body: if (condition) { ... }. Java is case-sensitive — IF is invalid. Unlike Python, there is no colon. The parentheses are not optional as they are in some other languages.

questions.question: What does the expression score >= 90 ? "A" : "B" evaluate to when score is 85?
options: true
A compile error
questions.explanation: The ternary operator evaluates the condition (score >= 90). Since 85 < 90, the condition is false, so the expression evaluates to the value after the colon — "B". The ternary operator is shorthand for a two-branch if/else and produces a value that can be assigned to a variable.

explanation.title: Comparing Values and Combining Conditions in Java
explanation.content:
# Comparison Operators

A condition inside an `if` statement must evaluate to a `boolean` — either `true` or `false`. Comparison operators produce this boolean result by comparing two values:

| Operator | Meaning | Example | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| `==` | Equal to | `5 == 5` | `true` |
| `!=` | Not equal to | `5 != 3` | `true` |
| `>` | Greater than | `7 > 3` | `true` |
| `<` | Less than | `3 < 7` | `true` |
| `>=` | Greater or equal | `5 >= 5` | `true` |
| `<=` | Less or equal | `4 <= 3` | `false` |

**Critical rule:** Use `==` only for primitives. For `String` comparison, always use `.equals()`:

```java
"hello" == "hello"         // unreliable — compares references
"hello".equals("hello")    // always correct — compares content
```

## Logical Operators

Combine multiple boolean conditions:

| Operator | Meaning | Short-circuits? |
|---|---|---|
| `&&` | AND — both must be true | Yes — skips right if left is false |
| `||` | OR — at least one must be true | Yes — skips right if left is true |
| `!` | NOT — flips true/false | N/A |

```java
int age    = 25;
boolean hasId = true;

if (age >= 18 && hasId) {
    System.out.println("Access granted.");
}

if (!hasId || age < 18) {
    System.out.println("Access denied.");
}
```

## Short-Circuit Evaluation

With `&&`, if the left operand is `false`, Java never evaluates the right operand. With `||`, if the left is `true`, the right is skipped. This is called **short-circuit evaluation** and prevents errors:

```java
// Safe: if s is null, s.length() is never called
if (s != null && s.length() > 0) {
    System.out.println("Non-empty string.");
}
```

## Operator Precedence

In an expression with multiple operators:
1. `!` (NOT) — highest
2. `>`, `<`, `>=`, `<=` — comparisons
3. `==`, `!=` — equality checks
4. `&&` (AND)
5. `||` (OR) — lowest

Use parentheses to make complex conditions clear:
```java
if ((a > b && b > c) || isSpecialCase) { ... }
```

In the code example below, you will see logical operators used to build a complete bank loan eligibility checker.

codeExample.title: Loan Eligibility Checker
codeExample.explanation:
- `age >= 21 && age <= 60` — both comparisons must be true; short-circuits on first false
- `!hasLoan` — `!` flips `false` to `true`; eligible only when no existing loan exists
- `creditRating.equals("Good") || creditRating.equals("Excellent")` — `.equals()` compares String content; `||` needs only one true
- `ageOk && incomeOk && loanOk && creditOk` — all four must be true for loan approval
- `name != null && name.length() > 0` — short-circuit safety; if `name` is `null`, `.length()` is never called, preventing a NullPointerException

exercise.title: Build a Platform Access Validator
exercise.instructions:
Write a Java program that validates access to an online platform. Declare: int age = 16, boolean hasParentConsent = true, String membershipType = "free". Using logical operators, determine: isAgeValid (age >= 13), canAccess (isAgeValid && (age >= 18 || hasParentConsent)), isPremium (membershipType.equals("premium")). Print each boolean result and a final access message.

Expected: isAgeValid=true, canAccess=true, isPremium=false, access granted.
hints: 💡 Add an if/else block at the end: if (canAccess) { System.out.println("Access granted."); } else { ... }
💡 canAccess is already computed — just use it as the condition in your if statement.
💡 All the boolean logic is already done; you only need to print a message based on the canAccess result.

questions.question: What is short-circuit evaluation in Java's && operator?
options: Java evaluates both operands before checking the result
If the left operand is false, Java skips evaluating the right operand
The && operator only works with boolean primitives
Java evaluates the right operand first to improve performance
questions.explanation: With &&, if the left operand evaluates to false, the entire expression is already false regardless of the right operand. Java skips evaluating the right side entirely. This is useful for safety checks like obj != null && obj.method() — if obj is null, the method call is never made and no NullPointerException occurs.

questions.question: Why should you not use == to compare two String objects in Java?
options: == does not compile for reference types
== compares memory references, not character content, and may return false for equal strings
== is slower than .equals() for strings
== only works when both strings are literals
questions.explanation: For objects like String, == compares whether both variables point to the exact same object in memory. Two String objects containing identical characters can exist at different memory locations, causing == to return false even though their content is the same. .equals() always compares the actual character sequences, reliably returning true for equal content.

explanation.title: Branching on Exact Values with Java's switch Statement

codeExample.title: Course Plan Feature Selector
codeExample.explanation:
- `switch (plan)` — evaluates `plan` once; jumps to the matching `case` label
- `case "bundle":` without `break` — intentional fall-through; bundle plan first prints extra features, then falls into `pro`
- `break` — required to exit the switch; without it execution continues into the next case
- `int courseCount = switch (plan) { case "bundle" -> 4; ... }` — switch expression assigns a value; no `break` needed
- `case 'A' -> "Outstanding!"` — arrow syntax; no fall-through, no `break`, produces a value directly
- `default` — handles any value not matched by an explicit case; always include it

exercise.title: Build a Month Name Converter
exercise.instructions:
Write a Java program that converts a month number to its name using a switch expression (Java 14+ style). Declare int month = 8. Use a switch expression that maps 1 to 'January', 2 to 'February', ... 8 to 'August', and groups 9-12 by using separate arrow cases. For default return 'Invalid month'. Print 'Month 8 is: August'.

Expected output: Month 8 is: August
hints: 💡 Add case 8 -> "August"; after the July case using the same arrow syntax pattern.
💡 Add cases 9 through 12 with their month names, each on its own case line.
💡 The default is already written — it handles any number outside 1-12.

questions.question: What happens in a classic switch statement if you forget to add break at the end of a case?
options: The program throws a runtime exception
Only the matching case runs — break is optional
Execution falls through and runs the next case's code too
The compiler generates an error
questions.explanation: Without break, execution falls through from the matched case into the next case, running its code too — and continues falling through until either a break or the end of the switch is reached. This is sometimes intentional (grouping cases), but is usually a bug. Arrow-style switch expressions (Java 14+) never fall through.

questions.question: Which of the following types CANNOT be used as a switch expression in Java?
options: String
int
double
char
questions.explanation: Java's switch statement supports int, byte, short, char, String, and enums as the switch expression. double and float are not supported because floating-point equality comparisons are unreliable due to precision issues. long is also not directly supported in switch.

questions.question: What advantage does the switch expression arrow syntax (case x -> value) have over classic switch?
options: It runs faster than classic switch
It eliminates fall-through and does not require break statements
It supports more data types than classic switch
It can replace all if/else statements
questions.explanation: Arrow-case switch expressions (Java 14+) do not fall through between cases, so break statements are not needed. Each arrow case is isolated. They also produce a value that can be assigned to a variable or returned from a method, making them more expressive than classic switch statements.
